notes: Riverboat guests. Empress visitors are typically warmer than the Magician's or Priestess's — they came for dinner. The boat is moored to the same piling it has been moored to for nine years; the river under the hull does the thinking. The hard moods that count for win conditions are gruff (which lands less often on the boat) and preoccupied (which lands when somebody is here because they had nowhere else to go).

visitors.name: The Neighbor
visitors.lore_text: Lives on the next boat over — a smaller boat, a one-room cabin Nicola helped them rebuild after the river took half of it in '94. They come for tea whenever the kettle's on, which is most evenings. They have been doing this for as long as either of them can remember. Nicola has not asked their birthday in eleven years.

steps.greet: Wave from the gangway. They wave back. The wave is the whole greeting. Either of you could write the wave down and the other would know which one this was.
steps.listen: They tell you about the morning's birds. The story is short and well-shaped. They have been honing it since seven a.m.
steps.deliver: You bring tea — the loose-leaf from the pantry, the tin Nicola's grandmother brought back from one of the trips that ended the same way every trip ended. They take it without saying thank you. They don't need to. They are inside the kind of friendship where thank-you is the wrong shape of the gesture.
steps.sit_with: You sit on the lounge bench together. The bank goes by even though the boat doesn't move. They lean their shoulder into yours for a moment. You both watch the river be the river.

visitors.name: The First Arrivals
visitors.lore_text: The couple who always shows up half an hour early because they want to help. They have helped enough now that they know where the salt is, where the small forks live, which drawer holds the placemats with the river-pattern. Their names are Helen and Bill. They have been married for forty-one years. They are the kind of couple who finish each other's stories not because they share a brain but because they have been making the stories together long enough to know which pieces belong to whom.

steps.greet: They are already setting the table by the time you turn around. Helen has the forks. Bill has the napkins. They cross-step around each other in the galley without speaking.
steps.listen: One of them tells the story. The other tells the corrections. The story is about a chair that broke at a wedding in 1989. The corrections are gentle.
steps.deliver: You bring the wine — a bottle the boat has been holding for a year and a season, the cork breaking cleanly the way the boat's corks usually do because of how Nicola stores them. They taste, nod, refill yours first.
steps.sit_with: You sit down. They have set your place at the head. They have set their two places adjacent to yours, on either side, the way people who have been to your boat enough times know to.

visitors.name: The Son Visiting
visitors.lore_text: A son in his thirties, back to see his mother on the boat. He lives in a planned community two states over now — the kind of place Alma's volume describes, sixty-eight degrees year-round by corporate decree. He has not been here in two years. He is uncomfortable. He doesn't know if it's the boat or the visit or the planned community he just drove eight hours from. The boat has not changed and he has, and he has not yet figured out whether the boat is the kind one or he is.

steps.greet: He thanks you for having him. The thanks is too formal for the room. He has been rehearsing the visit since exit 47.
steps.listen: He talks about his work. He's careful with it. He's careful with everything tonight. He uses the word 'community' three times and you can hear him decide each time that you'll know what he means.
steps.deliver: You bring him a small plate of the first crop — tomato and basil from the upper deck, two slices of bread, a piece of cheese his mother used to keep. He eats slowly. He says it tastes like he remembers. He says it as if he hadn't planned to say it.
steps.sit_with: You sit with him at the long table. He puts his elbow on the table and then takes it off and then puts it back. He stays. He is going to leave soon. He is going to come back sooner than two years next time, and he is going to know that he is going to before he leaves tonight.

visitors.name: The River Widow
visitors.lore_text: Lost her husband to the river twenty-six years ago, on a boat much smaller than this one. Now lives in a small house on the bank, with a window that faces the dock so she can see the boat from her kitchen. Comes to the boat once a season because the boat is the closest she has to him. She does not say his name aloud anymore. She has not for nineteen years. Nicola knows it. Nicola has never said it either.

steps.greet: She nods. She does not say his name. She does not need to. The boat has had her name in the registry of regulars for a long time.
steps.listen: She tells you about a meal she made for him once. The meal was thirty years ago. She still remembers the seasoning. She remembers which kitchen she made it in (his mother's, after the funeral for somebody else). She tells the meal the way a person tells a meal that has held them together since.
steps.deliver: You bring her the dish you made tonight in the shape she described. The shape is the shape. The taste is yours, but the shape is hers and his. She closes her eyes when she takes the first bite. She opens them slowly.
steps.sit_with: You sit with her at the bow window. The river splits around the hull there — one half left, one half right, the same way it always has, the way it did when her husband was on it. She is calm with it. She is more calm than she would be in her own kitchen. The boat does the thing the boat does for her.

visitors.name: Cousin from Upriver
visitors.lore_text: Family from the next town up the river. Brings news that the family in this town doesn't have yet. The news is mostly fine. They smell faintly of fish because they kept the wharfs in their hometown the way Nicola kept the boat in hers, and the smell is in their coat collar the way the boat's smell is in Nicola's.

steps.greet: Big hug at the gangway. They smell faintly of fish and faintly of a perfume Nicola's mother used to wear.
steps.listen: They tell you everything. The third cousin is engaged. The aunt is selling the house on the hill. The river up there is at the same level it has been all summer. The bank has a small slip on the curve below the church that everybody knows is going to give but hasn't yet.
steps.deliver: You bring them coffee. They take it black and forget about it after one sip. They are the kind of family that forgets coffee within a sentence.
steps.sit_with: You sit at the long table. They have everyone caught up with everyone by the end. The room is the size of a year's worth of family by the time they're done.

visitors.name: The Drift
visitors.lore_text: Late arrival, off the river. No name they want you to use. They drifted up to the gangway in a small flat-bottomed boat with no name on it either, and asked if it would be ok if they came aboard for a meal. You said yes. You always say yes. Nicola's grandmother said yes every time and Nicola has not seen a reason to say no yet. The drifter is wearing a coat too thin for the season.

steps.greet: They nod. They will not look at you long. They look at the floor for a moment when they cross the threshold, the way someone looks at a floor that is the first floor they have stood on in a while.
steps.listen: They say very little. What they do say lands — it lands the way a small heavy thing lands on a soft surface, with the soft surface giving exactly the right amount.
steps.deliver: You bring them whatever you have. They eat it. They do not comment on the food, which Nicola has learned is its own kind of compliment.
steps.sit_with: You sit at the guest cabin's narrow seat. They are quiet. You are quiet. The boat is quiet. The river isn't. The river hasn't been quiet in a hundred years.

visitors.name: Dante (visiting)
visitors.lore_text: Dante from three doors down on the bank. His office faces the dock. He came over for dinner the way he has been coming over for dinner since he and Nicola were children. He sits in the wheelhouse because the wheelhouse has a window in every direction and he is a man who needs to see what's coming. He has known Nicola since the boat was somebody else's boat. He is the Emperor's hand in vol5 ch4. Tonight he is a guest.

steps.greet: He thanks you for the invitation, which you didn't extend. He invites himself when he needs to. He has been doing this for thirty-six years.
steps.listen: He talks about the bank — the actual riverbank, not the institution. He talks about the way it has changed since the magician's steamboat got going. He uses the word 'sinkhole' twice without quite naming it.
steps.deliver: You bring him bread, butter, a glass of the wine. He notices each one. He nods his thanks the way somebody who has signed eight thousand documents nods their thanks for one of them.
steps.sit_with: You sit in the wheelhouse together. He doesn't touch the wheel. He looks at the river the way a person looks at a thing that is going to keep being there — appraising it, the way he appraises a paper before he stamps it. The radio in the wheelhouse hums in standby.

visitors.name: Nicola (visiting)
visitors.lore_text: When the player is NOT Nicola, she comes back to the boat off-shift — from a long afternoon ashore, the kind that happens when she's checked on the neighbor's boat and made it back without anyone asking her to. When the player IS Nicola, this entry swaps out and her cousin steps onto the gangway instead.

steps.greet: She steps onto her own gangway and pretends she doesn't know everything in the room. The pretense is generous; the kitchen smells like the meal she planned this morning.
steps.listen: She tells you about the second-cousin who is in town for a week. She tells it like a person who has known the boat hold three of those weeks.
steps.deliver: You bring her bread + butter + the herbs you cut from the upper deck. She takes a small piece of bread and tears it the way her grandmother tore it.
steps.sit_with: You sit with her at the long table. She breathes out for the first time in a long time. The boat steadies. The boat does that when she sits down. The boat has been doing that since before she was born.

visitors.name: Frasier (visiting)
visitors.lore_text: Frasier from the cathedral comes over to the boat for dinner. He brings a small balsa-wood model — sometimes a chair, sometimes the boat itself in 1:48 scale. He has been making the boat for nine years and the boat has not yet been finished. He has been making the diner for fifteen and that one is finished. Tonight's model is a tomato. The tomato is the size of his thumb. It is painted exactly the right red.

steps.greet: He hands you a small balsa-wood object as a gift. It is a model of a tomato. He has painted it tonight's exact red.
steps.listen: He says: 'I keep making the boat. I don't know why.' He says it the way a person says something they have figured out only as the sentence leaves them.
steps.deliver: You bring him the herbs and the bread. He smells the herbs and his face does the thing his face does when something lines up.
steps.sit_with: You sit with him at the long table. He eats slowly. He is full before the third course. He stays anyway. He sits with the model in his hand the whole meal. The model does not get put down. The boat does not get put down either, the way some things don't.
